coredata.dc:title: Fluorescent carbon dots for highly sensitive bilirubin sensing with excellent selectivity
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Science: Advanced Materials and Devices
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 Vietnam National University, Hanoi. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: Vietnam National University, Hanoi. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Sasikumar, Kandasamy
Rajamanikandan, Ramar
Ju, Heongkyu
coredata.dc:description: We reported the fluorescence sensor of carbon dots (CDs) synthesized for the highly sensitive and selective detection of bilirubin in human urine. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images showed that the CDs have a spherical shape with a mean particle size of 2.6 nm. The CDs showed the fluorescent emission, which peaked at the wavelength of 511 nm under the excitation wavelength of 435 nm. The CDs’ emission intensity reduced with increasing the bilirubin concentration, which was ascribed to the strong inner filter effect (IFE). The excellent spectral match between the bilirubin absorbance and the CDs excitation allowed the fluorescence sensor to be markedly specific only to the presence of bilirubin, permitting the identification of bilirubin even in the presence of other potentially interfering elements. The fluorescence sensor displayed a good linear response to the bilirubin concentration in the wide range of 3.5–45.5 μM with a limit of detection (LOD) of 34 nM, being the remarkably low level even though CDs were synthesized in a facile way without doping. Moreover, this sensing strategy permitted us to quantify bilirubin in the clinical (real) specimen with a good recovery of 95.3–96.8%, indicating a great potential candidate for a cost-effective bilirubin sensing platform in a biomedical application.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Bilirubin
Biosensor
Carbon dots
Facile synthesis
Fluorescence quenching
Inner filter effect

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Tests of sensor quantification accuracy with a human urine specimen
Sensitive detection of bilirubin using CDs

xocs:cp-license-lines.xocs:cp-license-line: This is an open access article under the CC BY license.

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2023 Vietnam National University, Hanoi. Published by Elsevier B.V.

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Science, ICT and Future Planning

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) grant funded by the Korean government (MSIT) (No. NRF-2021M3H4A3A02086939 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) HRTEM image of CDs, (b) HRTEM image of single CD shows the lattice fringes, and (c) Histogram shows size distribution of CDs.
(a) The XPS full scan spectrum of CDs. The XPS high-resolution spectra of (b) C 1s (c) N 1s, and (d) O 1s of CDs.

(a) Absorbance spectrum and (b) Emission spectrum of CDs. The inset displays the photos of CDs in cuvette cells - (i) colorless in day light (ii) bright greenish blue color under UV light (λ = 365 nm).

(a) Fluorescence spectra of CDs mixed with various concentrations of bilirubin (from 0 to 45.5 μM (a–n) in a step of 3.5 μM). The inset shows the CDs in cuvette cells (i) without bilirubin and (ii) with bilirubin (45.5 μM) added, (b) The amount of reduction of fluorescence peak intensity with increasing bilirubin concentration (The solid line represents the linear regression to fit the data).

(a) Normalized spectra of excitation/emission of CDs and bilirubin absorption, (b) Time-resolved fluorescence decay profile of CDs without and with bilirubin added (40 μM). The samples were excited at the wavelength of 435 nm and the emission wavelength was set to 511 nm.
The IFE mechanism for fluorescence quenching.
The CDs fluorescence quenching amount ΔF (=F0–F) when bilirubin (45.5 μM) or various biomolecules (225 μM each) were added (excitation light of λ = 365 nm).
A schematic representation of the synthesis of CDs and bilirubin sensing.
Comparison of bilirubin sensing using CDs synthesized by various methods.

ce:article-footnote.ce:note-para: Peer review under responsibility of Vietnam National University, Hanoi.

head.ce:title: Fluorescent carbon dots for highly sensitive bilirubin sensing with excellent selectivity

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Gachon University, Seongnam-si, Gyeonggi-do, 13120, Republic of Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics, Gachon University, Seongnam-si, Gyeonggi-do, 13120, Republic of Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We reported the fluorescence sensor of carbon dots (CDs) synthesized for the highly sensitive and selective detection of bilirubin in human urine. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images showed that the CDs have a spherical shape with a mean particle size of 2.6 nm. The CDs showed the fluorescent emission, which peaked at the wavelength of 511 nm under the excitation wavelength of 435 nm. The CDs’ emission intensity reduced with increasing the bilirubin concentration, which was ascribed to the strong inner filter effect (IFE). The excellent spectral match between the bilirubin absorbance and the CDs excitation allowed the fluorescence sensor to be markedly specific only to the presence of bilirubin, permitting the identification of bilirubin even in the presence of other potentially interfering elements. The fluorescence sensor displayed a good linear response to the bilirubin concentration in the wide range of 3.5–45.5 μM with a limit of detection (LOD) of 34 nM, being the remarkably low level even though CDs were synthesized in a facile way without doping. Moreover, this sensing strategy permitted us to quantify bilirubin in the clinical (real) specimen with a good recovery of 95.3–96.8%, indicating a great potential candidate for a cost-effective bilirubin sensing platform in a biomedical application.

ce:section.ce:para:
Bilirubin is a yellow color tetrapyrrole compound, largely generated from the catabolism of hemoglobin found in senescent red blood cells by a reticuloendothelial system, especially the liver and spleen [1,2]. In normal physiological circumstances, bilirubin is conjugated with glucuronic acid (or direct) in the liver to create a water–soluble complex and then excreted in bile and urine, while the unconjugated (or indirect) form of bilirubin is known as a potentially toxic. The maximum concentration of bilirubin in normal blood sera is about 17.1 μM. A build-up of bilirubin level
≥
34.2 μM renders the physiological condition called hyperbilirubinemia, which causes jaundice with the signs of yellowish skin and sclera [3–5]. It may also lead to severe diseases such as hepatitis, hemolysis, and cirrhosis. Conversely, a low concentration of bilirubin is related to coronary artery disease and anemia [6]. Hence, accurate quantification of the bilirubin level is clinically significant, and developing a facile and rapid analytical method for bilirubin detection is a prerequisite for health care.

Over the last few decades, various analytical methodologies for quantifying bilirubin levels in clinical specimens have been reported [7–10]. Spectrophotometry-based sensing technique is straightforward; meanwhile, limited by the intrusion of other types of pigments during spectral measurements [11]. The colorimetric diazo technique is cost-effective and practically simple to use, while being subject to the pH-dependence with its accuracy further restricted by the presence of other heme proteins [12–14]. Electrochemical detection techniques have been reported for bilirubin detection due to low detection limit, quick response time, and good reproducibility [7,15,16]. However, there are inherent limitations, such as the narrow operation range of temperature/humidity and limited lifespan. The enzymatic assay technique has also been reported as an effective one avoiding the interfering effects of other biological elements with relative ease of operation. These techniques, however, would face the challenges of insufficient stability and lack of reproducibility [17,18].

The fluorometry-based strategy for analyte detection has received renewed attention owing to the merits of very high sensitivity, selectivity, real-time response, cost-effective sensing processes in a simple format, a wide range of detectable concentrations of analytes, sensing reproducibility, and relatively high invulnerability to humidity and temperature variation [19–21]. Numerous nanomaterials capable of emitting fluorescent light, have been developed for biosensing, bioimaging, and drug delivery. These include the nanoparticles of silica-based materials, organic polymers, metal nanoclusters (NCs), quantum dots (QDs), and up-conversion materials [22–27]. Amid those, carbon quantum dots (typical size <10 nm), shortly carbon dots (CDs) received continuous interest due to the distinct advantages of high conductivity, good aqueous solubility, low toxicity, good biocompatibility, the excellent capability of surface engineering, and high quantum yield, unlike conventional semiconductor QDs [28,29]. The above-mentioned characteristics together with size-dependent tunability of emission wavelength and good photostability would make them a great candidate for the fluorescence sensor that can detect analytes such as disease biomarkers [30–32].

A number of techniques thus have been devised to synthesize fluorescent CDs, including laser ablation, arc discharge, chemical vapor deposition, chemical exfoliation, electrochemical, and microwave heating [33–38]. Most of those techniques require complex synthesis conditions, post-treatment processes, and expensive precursors, imposing challenges for producing processable fluorescent CDs in a large quantity. Thereby, a more simplistic synthesis of CDs has been reported with dl-malic acid [39,40]. It has also been reported that the CDs synthesized in this facile way were capable of sensing biological analytes such as thrombin, uric acid, and chlorogenic acid with some properties such as sensitivity and selectivity [41–43].

In this paper, we describe the quantitative detection of bilirubin in human urine using CDs as a fluorescence sensor (Scheme 1 ). The CDs were produced from dl-malic acid through a hydrothermal process. The CDs’ emission intensity was notably suppressed upon the addition of bilirubin through the inner filter effect (IFE) and static quenching processes, enabling a trace level of bilirubin to be quantified. According to the results, the CDs-based fluorescence sensor allowed the bilirubin to be detected in the range of 3.5–45.5 μM with a limit of detection (LOD) of 34 nM with excellent selectivity. This indicates that the CDs synthesized in such a facile way with dl-malic acid can even produce higher sensitivity and wider sensing range than CDs for bilirubin sensing reported elsewhere. This fact can open up a way to realize a potent real-time device for bilirubin detection with high sensitivity and excellent selectivity in a wide sensing range.

Analytical grade chemicals of bilirubin, dl-malic acid, N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF), dopamine (DA), glycine (Gly), ascorbic acid (AA), aspartic acid (Apa), uric acid (UA), urea, thiourea (TU), sodium chloride (NaCl), l-cysteine (Cys), glucose (Glu), l-glutathione reduced (GSH), cystamine dihydrochloride (Cyst), and bovine serum albumin (BSA) were procured from Sigma Aldrich, USA. The human urine specimen (diluent) was obtained from the same supplier. All the chemicals were directly utilized without extra purification. Highly cleansed deionized (DI) water was used all over the experiment.

The synthesis protocol of CDs via a hydrothermal approach was reported elsewhere [44]. At first, 0.01 M of malic acid was dissolved in 20 mL of DMF, and the solution was stirred at room temperature using a magnetic stirrer for 15 min. Then, 3 mL of DI water was gently added to the solution and sonicated for 30 min. This solution was shifted into the polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE-50 mL capacity) liner and kept in a stainless-steel autoclave. The reaction temperature and reaction time was fixed as 180 °C for 8 h. After cooling the solution to room temperature, a dialysis bag (500 Da) was utilized for 8 h to remove DMF, unreacted precursors, and other intermediate molecules. The obtained high-purity CDs were refrigerated at 4 °C.

The fluorescence measurements were conducted at room temperature using a spectrofluorometer (FS-2, SCINCO, South Korea). Absorption spectra of CDs were obtained by a spectrophotometer (V–770, JASCO, Japan) where quartz cuvettes of 1 cm path length were used. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were collected using a spectrometer (FTIR-4600, JASCO, Japan). The samples needed for high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) examination were made using the following procedure. We dissolved 10 μL of CDs colloidal solution in 15 mL of water and dispersed a few drops of the solution over a carbon-coated copper grid and sonicated it. After drying, the samples were studied using an HRTEM instrument (Tecnai G2 F30, FEI, USA) with an operating voltage of 300 kV. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis of CDs was executed using an instrument (Multilab-2000, Thermo Scientific, USA) with a monochromatic AlKα X-ray source (1486.6 eV) for excitation. Time-resolved fluorescence data were obtained using a time-correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) instrument (DeltaPro, Horiba Scientific, Japan) with a 390 nm laser as the excitation source. The zeta potential was analyzed using a zeta potential analyzer (ZetaPlus, Brookhaven Instruments, USA) with a 633 nm Helium–Neon laser fitted with an MPT-2 autotitrator.

Given that water-insoluble bilirubin becomes soluble in an alkaline solution, we prepared a bilirubin solution by dispersing 2.0 mg of bilirubin in 5 mL of DI water with 0.1 M of NaOH. A known concentration of this bilirubin solution was mixed with 0.5 mL of CDs in a flask, and the final volume of the solution was corrected to 5 mL by adding 0.01 M of phosphate-buffered saline (PBS, pH = 7.4) solution. The reaction mixture was equilibrated for 4 min. The fluorescence spectrum was then collected at room temperature at an excitation wavelength of 435 nm. A similar procedure was carried out to assess the selectivity of CDs towards bilirubin over a series of other interferents such as Na+, Gly, AA, DA, UA, Cys, GSH, Cyst, Apa, urea, TU, Glu, and BSA. Measurement of fluorescence for detecting each analyte was repeated at least five times at room temperature (25 °C).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Tests of sensor quantification accuracy with a human urine specimen
ce:section.ce:para: The quantification accuracy of the sensor was tested with a human urine specimen. First, a known concentration (e.g. 10 μM) of bilirubin was spiked with a urine specimen that already included a known trace level of bilirubin (0.61 μM). The spiked urine was then mixed with the colloidal suspension of CDs, followed by the solution equilibration for 4 min. Finally, the fluorescence spectra of the spiked urine were collected in the wavelength range of 450–650 nm under the excitation wavelength of 435 nm. A linear relation between reduction of fluorescence peak intensity and added concentration of bilirubin permitted the sensor to reconstruct its concentration for its known actual level, giving the quantification accuracy referred to as the “recovery” whereby 100% would represent perfect reconstruction of added concentration [45].

The morphology and size of CDs were examined by HRTEM analysis. The HRTEM image shown in Fig. 1 a displays the spherical-shaped CDs with an excellent dispersibility. Fig. 1b shows the lattice fringe spacing of 0.21 nm which is attributed to (100) plane of the graphitic carbon [46]. The size distribution histogram is displayed in Fig. 1c, demonstrating the reasonably uniform distribution in the range of 1.8–3.8 nm with a mean particle size of 2.6 nm.

The FTIR spectral analysis was executed to verify the existence of functional groups on CDs. As shown in Fig. S1, the peak centered at 3438 cm−1 indicates the ─OH/NH stretching vibration. The narrow peaks at 1096 cm−1 and 659 cm−1 exhibit the presence of C─O─C bonds and out of plane bending of ─OH bonds, respectively [47]. The absorption peaks at 1642 cm−1 and 2984 cm−1 are related to C─O and C─H stretching vibrations, respectively [48]. The peaks at 1176 cm−1 and 1412 cm−1 are assigned to C─O and C─N stretching vibrations [44,49]. The peak at 881 cm−1 is attributed to bending vibration between C─H and para-substitution C─N in aromatic ring.

The elemental compositions and chemical states of CDs were examined through XPS spectral analysis. As shown in Fig. 2 a, the full scan spectrum of CDs displays the three specific peaks at 285.27, 400.54, and 532.15 eV, which can be attributed to C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s signals, respectively. The presence of N is due to the use of DMF solvent during synthesis. In the high-resolution C 1s spectrum (Fig. 2b), the C 1s band can be deconvoluted into three peaks of C─C/C─C at 284.81 eV, C─O/C─N at 286.35 eV, and C─O at 288.41 eV [50]. The main peak at 284.81 eV is ascribed to the formation of graphitic carbon. This peak obviously indicates that the synthesized CDs predominantly possess sp2 carbons [51]. The N 1s spectrum (Fig. 2c) can be divided into two peaks, which are related to pyrrolic N (400 eV) and graphitic N (402.21 eV) [52]. The O 1s spectrum (Fig. 2d) contains two peaks at 531.5 and 533.11 eV, corresponding to C─O and C─O groups, respectively [44]. The surface components and core structure details achieved from XPS studies are consistent with the FTIR spectral data.

The optical properties of as-synthesized CDs were studied by UV-vis absorption spectroscopy. The absorption spectrum of CDs exhibits one distinct absorbance peak at 310 nm. Additionally, it also shows a peak at 347 nm and a local wide band of absorbance centered at 372 nm, which are shown in Fig. 3 a. These result from the n–π∗ transitions of C─O group in the CDs’ structure [53,54]. The emission spectrum of CDs shows a sharp peak at 511 nm under the excitation wavelength of 435 nm (Fig. 3b). As displayed in the inset of Fig. 3b, the CDs were colorless in day light while emitting light of bright greenish blue color under UV illumination of 365 nm wavelength.

The CDs' photostability investigation revealed that the CDs’ fluorescence spectrum remained unchanged even after the CDs were exposed to the UV light of 365 nm for 120 min (Fig. S2), indicating their excellent photostability. In addition, this fluorescence stability remained valid after the CDs were aged for one month.

The influence of pH on the fluorescence properties of CDs mixed with bilirubin (7 μM) was examined in a wide pH ranging from 4 to 10. As shown in Fig. S3a, the bilirubin-induced reduction in fluorescence peak (511 nm) intensity of CDs became substantial in the range of pH 6−8 with its maximum reduction occurring at pH = 7. This demonstrated the use of the CDs-based sensing strategy to be suitable for a normal physiological condition at pH 7.4. This also led us to use pH 7.4 in PBS for further sensing experiments.

The bilirubin (7 μM)-induced quenching of CDs fluorescence was also studied as a function of (reaction) time by recording the fluorescence peak intensity for 20 min at every 2-min step at room temperature, which is shown in Fig. S3b. It was unveiled that the significant reaction of CDs with bilirubin took place in about 4 min, beyond which no further marked change occurred. Hence, the reaction time of 4 min could be selected as the reaction equilibrium time, determining the sensing time threshold of this sensing strategy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitive detection of bilirubin using CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Under the optimized conditions mentioned above, we performed the quantitative detection of bilirubin via the CDs fluorescence. Various bilirubin concentrations (3.5–45.5 μM) were prepared through titration for being introduced into the CDs in PBS solution with a pH of 7.4. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the CDs fluorescence reduced with increasing bilirubin concentration. Before bilirubin addition, the fluorescence spectrum peaked at 511 nm under excitation light of the 435 nm wavelength. Upon adding bilirubin of 3.5 μM, the intensity of the fluorescence spectral peak reduced with the slight red shift of the peak wavelength. A sequentially added aliquot of bilirubin continually decreased the peak intensity. When the bilirubin concentration reached 45.5 μM, nearly 96% of the intensity was quenched with the concomitant spectral peak red shift of about 15 nm. Fluorescence quenching with increasing bilirubin could also be easily verified by visual check of the solution in cuvette cell under the UV light illumination (λ = 365 nm), i.e., change from greenish blue to colorless, as exhibited in the inset of Fig. 4a.

This bilirubin-induced fluorescence quenching was owing to the creation of the non-fluorescent complex comprising CDs and bilirubin. The decrease in peak intensity for a given bilirubin concentration was quantified by ΔF = F0–F where F0 and F represent the fluorescence peak intensity without and with bilirubin added, respectively. Measured data of ΔF versus bilirubin concentration were fitted with a linear regression (R2 = 0.9768) in the range of 3.5–45.5 μM, as shown in Fig. 4b. The linear relation in a reasonably long range could form a basis for quantitative detection of bilirubin, producing the LOD of 34 nM, the concentration corresponding to three times the blank signal (without bilirubin case) [55]. It is worth stating that apart from the facile synthesis of fluorescent CDs without sulfur or nitrogen doping, the LOD value was comparable to those obtained with CDs reported elsewhere, as shown in Table 1 .

As shown in Fig. S4, the value of zeta potential decreased from −19.1 mV to −9.95 mV to −3.71 mV as the bilirubin concentration increased from zero to 20 μM–40 μM. This indicates that the denser layers of bilirubin molecules surrounding the CDs were constructed. In general, when quencher molecules are in proximity to fluorophores, fluorescence quenching can take place in various ways. These include Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET), photo-induced electron transfer (PET), and inner filter effect (IFE), etc. [63,64]. First, we assumed a possibility of fluorescence quenching due to the FRET between bilirubin molecules and CDs. However, the CDs emission spectrum overlapped negligibly with the bilirubin absorption spectrum as shown in Fig. 5 a, expecting nearly no FRET effect [65]. This could be supported by the time-resolved fluorescence data shown in Fig. 5b, which compared the fluorescence lifetime ( τ ) of CDs without and with bilirubin added (40 μM). Both cases produced a similar τ , i.e., 6.72 ns (without bilirubin) and 6.03 ns (with bilirubin), unfolding negligible FRET effect involved in the fluorescence quenching.

Second, we could assume a possibility of photo-induced electron transfer for fluorescence quenching. However, given the fact that the bilirubin molecules were electron-rich with a lone pair of electrons on nitrogen atoms in the pyrrole ring and the CDs are also electron-rich, all types of electron transfer could be excluded to account for quenching.

As shown in Fig. 5a, the bilirubin absorption spectrum overlapped significantly with the CDs excitation spectrum even with their peaks coinciding at 435 nm. This indicated the bilirubin molecules absorbed the excitation light, acting as barrier molecules surrounding CDs to prevent the excitation light from reaching the inner CDs. This effect referred to as the IFE in the excitation channel which would result in fluorescence reduction [66,67]. Unlike a typical IFE caused by highly concentrated fluorophores that would yield fluorescence with its intensity nonlinear with concentration, the fluorescence reduction mentioned above produced a linear relation with bilirubin concentration. This difference was due to the following: The fluorescence suffering a typical IFE of highly concentrated fluorophores in liquid was governed by fluorophores excited by surface-facing excitation light. In contrast, the fluorescence in the present work emitted from “inner” CDs excited by excitation light that was attenuated during penetration through the bilirubin barrier with its optical density proportional to bilirubin concentration. The IFE mechanism for fluorescence quenching involved in bilirubin sensing is displayed in Fig. 6 . Moreover, to check the morphology changes of CDs after the bilirubin addition, HRTEM investigation was performed and the corresponding micrograph is given in Fig. S5. As depicted in Fig. S5, an increased particle size with an aggregated structure was noticed, which strongly supports the ground state complex formation and bilirubin affected the size/morphology of the CDs.

The insignificant bilirubin-induced change in fluorescence lifetime as mentioned above specified that the fluorescence reduction was subject to the static (steady-state) quenching as described by the Stern–Volmer equation [68,69],
(1)
F 0
F
= 1 +
K
S V
[
b i l
]
= 1 +
K q
τ
[
b i l
]
where KSV is the Stern–Volmer quenching constant, [bil] is the bilirubin concentration, Kq is the time-differential quenching constant, and τ is the fluorescence lifetime of CDs without bilirubin (6.72 ns). Here, a good linear relationship was attained between F0/F and relatively low concentration range of bilirubin (Fig. S6). From the slope of the linear plot, the calculated values of KSV and Kq are 8.60 × 104 dm3 mol−1 and 1.28 × 1013 dm3 mol−1 s−1, respectively. The value of Kq turned out to be two-orders of magnitude larger than the maximum quenching constant (collision-based quenching) of 2× 1010 dm3 mol−1 s−1, verifying the creation of ground state complex for static quenching. Quantum yield (φ) was also calculated for CDs using rhodamine 6G aqueous solution (φ = 0.95) as a reference standard. Adding bilirubin decreased φ from 3.45% to 0.014% due to the IFE-induced quenching.

The bilirubin selectivity of the fluorescence-based sensor was put to test using other kinds of biomolecules commonly treated in biochemical engineering, such as Na+, Gly, AA, DA, UA, Cys, GSH, Cyst, Apa, urea, TU, Glu, and BSA. These biomolecules could be potential interferents against specific detection of bilirubin in the biomedical industry. Fig. 7 shows the fluorescence quenching amount ΔF with the corresponding visual color of the colloidal CDs mixed with biomolecules for comparison. The concentration of each kind of biomolecule was 225 μM, even 5 times as high as bilirubin (45.5 μM). Encouragingly, the fluorescence quenching occurred substantially only for bilirubin while strong fluorescence remained for other kinds, as vividly checked in the visual color of the colloidal CDs (inset of Fig. 7). This was because, except for bilirubin, the absorption bands of the other kinds did not significantly overlap with that of CDs, i.e., no IFE occurred for other kinds.

We applied the CDs-based fluorescence sensor to human urine specimen spiked with two different bilirubin concentrations, i.e., 10.00 and 20.00 μM for practical use test. The recovery value estimated from this fluorescence sensor are summarized in Table S1. The values of 95.3% and 96.8% with reasonably low value of the relative standard deviation represented the excellent sensor accuracy with a reasonable reproducibility for quantifying bilirubin in human urine.

The CDs of ∼2.6 nm size were hydrothermally synthesized in a facile way with dl-malic acid and the same were exploited as a fluorescence sensor for selectively quantifying bilirubin in biological fluids such as human urine. The CDs fluorescence reduction occurred when adding bilirubin, due to the IFE process for which the bilirubin absorption band overlapped with that of CDs. In the wide range of bilirubin concentration (3.5–45.5 μM), the linear nature of fluorescence quenching amount versus concentration of the bilirubin mixed with CDs, led to the sensing LOD of 34 nM, being the remarkably low level, even though the CDs were synthesized without doping in such a facile way. Time-resolved fluorescence study revealed that no substantial dynamic quenching was involved in the fluorescence reduction, but the stead-state quenching process dominated it. The IFE-induced quenching properties exclusively specific to bilirubin, which also provided excellent selectivity of bilirubin sensing against other kinds of biomolecules commonly used in biochemical and biomedical industries. The CDs-based fluorescence sensor was also demonstrated for its practical application to human urine specimen with excellent levels of recovery of bilirubin with good reproducibility. Hence, this can open up a promising path towards effective and selective sensing of bilirubin in human fluids using the facilely synthesized CDs.

Kandasamy Sasikumar: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Ramar Rajamanikandan: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Visualization. Heongkyu Ju: Validation, Investigation, Resources, Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) grant funded by the Korean government (MSIT) (No. NRF-2021M3H4A3A02086939).

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: T. Liu, Y. Zhang, T. Hou, Q. Xue, L. Wang, S. Wang, Sensitive fluorescent detection of fibrin based on the inner filter effect of gold nanoparticles, RSC Adv. 7 (2017) 23422-23426, https://doi.org/10.1039/c7ra02422c.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Inner filter effect in fluorescence spectroscopy: as a problem and as a solution
J. Photochem. Photobiol. C Photochem. Rev.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Kumar Panigrahi, A. Kumar Mishra, Inner filter effect in fluorescence spectroscopy: As a problem and as a solution, J. Photochem. Photobiol. C Photochem. Rev. 41 (2019) 100318, https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochemrev.2019.100318.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: More about the inner filter effect: corrections of Stern-Volmer fluorescence quenching constants are necessary at very low optical absorption of the quencher
J. Lumin.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: I.E. Borissevitch, More about the inner filter effect: corrections of Stern-Volmer fluorescence quenching constants are necessary at very low optical absorption of the quencher, J. Lumin. 81 (1999) 219-224, https://doi.org/10.1016/S0022-2313(98)00063-5.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Glutathione functionalized copper nanoclusters as a fluorescence platform for specific biosensing of cysteine and application in cellular imaging
Microchem. J.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Rajamanikandan, B. Aazaad, S. Lakshmipathi, M. Ilanchelian, Glutathione functionalized copper nanoclusters as a fluorescence platform for specific biosensing of cysteine and application in cellular imaging, Microchem. J. 158 (2020) 105253, https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2020.105253.
